item.title: Error in the exploration logs
item.content:encoded:
In Exploration log 5099.3, if not 5099.1 as well, sergeant Winslow and lieutenant Briggs's military ranks are inverted.

I don't think a sergeant would dare ask his lieutenant if he shit himself after a moderately scary episode.

item.title: Re: I like this
item.content:encoded: In the revised, second document, the object is categorised as two separate anomalies: the humanoid entity and the spear. The spear is classified as Thaumiel because it contains the entity.

item.title: I like this
item.content:encoded:
This is obviously NGE inspired and the logs are also cool

But why is this thaumiel?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With relatively few exceptions, radiometric dating only works on biological samples that are no longer alive.

Radiometric dating relies closely on the ability to determine what the ratio of isotopes in a sample was when it was created. This is fairly easy for biological samples, since the amounts of carbon-12 and carbon-14 in nature (and therefore in all living organisms) is pretty much constant over time, since carbon-14 is generated continuously by cosmic rays in the upper atmosphere.

I mentioned that carbon dating requires dead samples because living creatures are constantly exchanging carbon with the atmosphere (mainly by eating plants and breathing), and therefore maintain pretty much constant isotope ratios during their lifespans. For real creatures, this is relatively unimportant, since their lifespans are so short, but it's worth mentioning that you wouldn't be able to radiometrically date a living, ancient creature as long as that creature continues to exchange carbon with the atmosphere.

Beyond organic matter, it's much more challenging to radiometrically date inorganic samples that may have had unknown isotope concentrations when they were formed, and it's out of the question for samples whose composition is highly novel or undetermined. I don't believe its mentioned usage in this article is realistic, since the composition and origin of the object are stated to be unclear and vaguely extraterrestrial. Its isotope ratios at the time of its creation are totally unknown, so while we could determine what they are in the present by analyzing samples, we wouldn't have a reference point to determine how long the thing has existed.

item.title: The offset page links to the old page.
item.content:encoded:
title

wait shoot i replied to this post instead of making it its own thread i'm going to jail now and i can't delete my own replies for some reason

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well written and fits very well within the "Mystery" theme. You do still have an "Addendum XXXX.2" instead of an Addendum 5000.2" Apart from that, it's well made. This is a personal nitpick that doesn't detract from the quality, but "MTF Sigma-29 ("Lance of Longinus")" is pretty obvious and I would personally change it to something like "MTF Sigma-29 ("Crush")" or something along those lines. But that doesn't detract from the overall quality of this article. I'd be nothing but happy if this was chosen for the reservation of SCP-5000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love NGE to death, and I love 2317 to death, but this is pretty much just the lance from the former serving as the chain of the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well,

MTF Sigma-29 ("Lance of Longinus")

Makes it pretty clear when you have an impaled white giant godlike entity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a quick nitpick, the second addendum is still labeled "XXXX" instead of "5000." An enjoyable read otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You say double-helix that looks like a lance, and I think Neon Genesis Evangelion and nothing got me to stop thinking about it.

I felt like the writing got more rushed as this went on. The second iteration isn't as well edited as the first, and Morgenthau's journal in particular needed some work. I do like the general idea, of a giant weapon embedded in the earth keeping something horrible pinned down, but I'd like it to come out of the article more naturally via reader conjecture than just have it be told to me all at once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I am totally open to substantial edits once the contest is over:

Whilst the concept of the LoL served as massive inspiration for my scip, you can clearly tell that I've deviated away from the core concepts of Evangelion and have instead created a very different story from the one told in Hideaki Anno's amazing anime.

The answers you seek from the mystery are also, mostly, hinted at or outright told, some of the mysteries may require a little bit of guesswork to actually work out. Furthermore, some mysteries are better left as just that, mysteries.

Thank you for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personal nitpick, I don't enjoy it when a fairly well know piece of media, in this case, the pretty specific depiction of the Lance of Longinus from NGE, is lifted and re-used without some in universe reasoning. Feels to me a bit too much like pandering, and at worst, theft.

There were mysteries here that I wanted to know more about, if you are open to doing any substantial editing after the contest is over. I think the story could benefit from exploring, maybe just in hints, without spelling it out too quickly, some things like: why the carvings on the lance feature imagery whose sources (the creatures in evolutionary line, for example) did not exist at the time of the object's creation; whther the age of the lance, or the time of its arrival on earth are significant; why the entity is contained on earth, or why it happened to be on earth when it was contained (other than the fact that otherwise there wouldn't be a story); how Higgs' interactions with the entity or the lance might have contribited to the lance's deterioration (something to justify his appearance at the end as more than a creepypasta trope). The image of a powerful entity, in unstable containment is a really compelling one, which I'm sure is why there are so many examples of exactly that on this site. I just think this one needs more flavor to differentiate it from the others.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Response -->
<-- Thanks for critting! :)

Hey gee0765,

Firstly, thanks for reading my article through and giving me some feedback, it's all very useful in the process of writing this story.

To address some of the critique you've provided in your feedback, I totally agree with the second description being a little counter-intuitive and confused and will touch that up on a future update to the current article to make it less so.

Furthermore, I disagree with your first criticism about the initial four logs from the doctor, they serve to explain the history of the item in question.

The exploration logs are an exploration of the object's anomalous effects and also give us a little exploration of our four characters who have been sent in, alone, to deal with something that the Foundation doesn't even understand, but I do agree in part with the crit concerning them blending together in their current state. I think that is a problem that I'll go over and hopefully fix.

Finally, the reveal at the end isn't the existence of the creature, but the fact that it's containment procedure is failing. The lance that's got it impaled is slowly deteriorating, that's why the fragments were snapping off so easily as well. The Foundation is on limited time and it's only so long before the creature is freed.

As to the compulsion effects, it's not compulsion to the same degree as many other cosmic entities "forcing" their subjects to work towards them but rather that humans are being utilised and played with like human toys. The entity is a child-like being that's grasping out with its "hands" and that's what's twisting reality around them.

However, having said all this, I do agree that it could do with a rewrite to better clarify these intended outcomes and messages.

Overall, thank you very much for the critique!

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

I'm not especially impressed by this one. The weird actions of the doctor in the first iteration aren't especially interesting, and all the other researcher does is act as an explanation to the reader for if it was too subtle. Essentially, due to those last two logs, the first four by the doctor are mostly unnecessary to the story and far too long.

As for the second section, some aspects of the description are very weird. Descriptions of the entity as "immense" but also of "unknown size" is one of the issues, as is overuse of vague terms like 'gigantic' and inconsistency in the hyphenation of handprints (imo it should just be one word). The assertion that the being is alive in higher dimensions is also completely unfounded and not realistic. I'm also very unimpressed by the entity itself. It's just some sort of superpowered being which uses compulsion effects to free itself. There are several issues with compulsion effects outlined in this thread, several of which I believe apply here.

The exploration logs aren't really anything special. The majority of them is just confirming what we already know from the description and the characters don't really justify this. They all blend together and none really have many apparent traits. The final reveal was completely lost on me too, since we already knew about the existence of the creature. Just telling me that it's white doesn't change the fact that it's little more than a reality bender that's trapped. And the fact that the trap wasn't made by the foundation doesn't really change that for me; I'm still getting no real emotion or feeling out of this. Due to the uninteresting anomaly and not especially unique story, I'm led to downvote this.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you caught the references in the article!

NGE was a massive inspiration during the last few years of my life and I wanted to write something reflective of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know of this is intentional, but I keep thinking of Neon Genesis Evangelion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the corrections, I'll be sure to update the scip to better reflect this.

Does radiometric dating work on biological objects as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few corrections to make

For the first article: switch carbon dating to radiometric dating. Radiometric dating is the general term and carbon dating only works for objects less than 100,000 years old.

For the updated article: you mix up SCP-5000 and SCP-5000-1 a few times.

Overall besides those things I like this a decent amount. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It all returns to nothing,
it just keeps tumbling down, tumbling down, tumbling down.

Hi guys! Many thanks to everyone involved in this project!

Special thanks to Servatar, and everyone else involved with it.

For slots, I would kindly request:

  • 5995
  • 5099
  • 5012
  • 5666
  • 5001

The image used in this article comes from here under the CC BY 4.0 license.
